symbols: beam of light from upper right — divine grace breaking into the darkness of sin, God's initiative in calling
Christ's pointing hand — the irresistible call of grace, echoing God's creative gesture
coins on the table — the worldly attachments Matthew must leave behind
Matthew's self-pointing gesture — the astonishment of being chosen despite unworthiness
darkness of the room — the spiritual blindness from which Matthew is called into light
description: A dark interior scene dramatically lit by a shaft of light entering from the upper right. At the right edge, a standing figure in dark robes extends his right hand in a pointing gesture toward a group of five men seated around a table at left. The seated men are counting coins; one looks up and points to himself in surprise. The figures wear a mix of contemporary and ancient dress. The contrast between deep shadow and brilliant illumination is extreme, with most of the composition shrouded in darkness.
theological_description: The Calling of Saint Matthew (Matthew 9:9-13) is one of the most theologically charged moments in the Gospels. Jesus enters a tax office — a place of corruption and collaboration with Rome — and with a single word ('Follow me') transforms a despised publican into an apostle and evangelist. The dramatic light in this composition is not merely natural but sacramental: it represents the intrusion of divine grace into human darkness. Christ's pointing hand deliberately echoes the hand of God in the Creation of Adam, suggesting that the calling of a sinner is a new creation. Matthew's gesture of self-doubt ('Who, me?') captures the scandal of grace: God does not call the righteous but sinners (Mark 2:17). The coins on the table represent everything Matthew will abandon. Augustine saw in Matthew's calling the paradigm of every conversion: unexpected, unmerited, and totally transformative. The darkness that fills the room is the darkness of a world that does not yet know its Redeemer.

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus calling Matthew from the tax booth — the exact scene depicted
Mark's parallel account emphasizing that Jesus came to call sinners, not the righteous
The light shines in the darkness — the theological meaning of the dramatic illumination
God who said 'Let light shine out of darkness' has shone in our hearts — conversion as new creation through light
You were once darkness but now you are light in the Lord — Matthew's transformation from darkness to apostleship
